text: The dissection was started by taking down the inferior pulmonary ligament.
Subsequently, the pleura was opened over the posterior surface of the lung up to the apex and the lymph node dissection was performed.
After this, the inferior pulmonary vein was dissected and the basal segment and superior segment branches were exposed posteriorly.
The pulmonary artery is being exposed in the fissure posteriorly.
Now the lung is retracted posteriorly and the pleura is being opened on the vein anteriorly to expose these branches.
Usually the superior branch is not visible anteriorly.
At this point, a window was created to get around the basal segment vein.
Now the dissection is started in the fissure to expose the pulmonary artery and its basal and superior segment branches.
It is key to extract all of the lymph nodes and to skeletonize the vessels in order to prevent vascular and bronchial injuries.
Now the curved tip 30mm robotic vascular stapler is introduced through the posterior port.
Subsequently, the basal vein is transected using another load of the same robotic stapler, also introduced through the posterior port.
The bronchus is then dissected further while the lung is being retracted cephalad.
Ultimately, the bronchus is skeletonized and exposed in the fissure.
At this point, a curved tip blue load robotic stapler was introduced through the posterior port to transect the bronchus.
We prefer to use the blue rather than green stapler load on segmental bronchi.
This led to transection of all the critical structures of the segment.
The parenchymal resection margins are selected in the line of the bronchus.
At this point, a 45 mm straight robotic stapler green load is introduced through the posterior port to perform the parenchymal resection.
In the author's experience, it usually takes one less staple load fired if using the robotic stapler instead of the traditional manual stapler.
The specimen was then placed in a bag and removed through the assistant port.
